beats.text: Open weights are just a folder of tensors and a config, inert until an inference engine loads and runs them.
The stack has four layers: hardware sets the memory ceiling, kernels run the matmul, the engine decides quant and cache, and the front-end is how you talk to it.
Two questions choose the engine: where the model lives, and how many requests run at once.
Single-stream decode is bound by memory bandwidth, not FLOPs, because every weight is read from memory for each token.

visual.note: EQ OM2.1 the decode bandwidth bound

beats.text: A 7B model in 4-bit reads about 3.5 GB per token, so a 1 TB/s GPU caps near 285 tokens per second.
Quantizing from 16-bit to 4-bit reads four times fewer bytes per token, so it roughly quadruples decode speed.
llama.cpp, started in 2023, is the dependency-light C and C++ engine that made local inference mainstream.
GGUF is a single self-describing file holding the quantized tensors, tokenizer, chat template, and metadata, so nothing can mismatch.
A name like Q4_K_M reads as roughly four bits per weight, k-quant per-block scales, and a medium mix keeping sensitive tensors higher.
Footprint is just parameters times bits per weight over eight, the whole game on a memory-limited box.
For a 7B model that means 14 GB at bf16, 7 GB at Q8, and 3.5 GB at Q4.
Apple unified memory shares one pool, so a 64 GB Mac can hold a 70B Q4 that no consumer discrete GPU can.
vLLM optimizes the opposite end from llama.cpp: many concurrent users on datacenter GPUs at the lowest cost per token.
Naive serving pre-allocates a contiguous cache per request at maximum length, stranding most of that memory.
PagedAttention borrows virtual memory: the cache becomes fixed-size blocks handed out on demand and shared across identical prefixes.

visual.note: EQ OM2.3 KV blocks and concurrency

beats.text: The original paper reports up to two to four times higher throughput at the same latency.
Throughput rises with batch size while the GPU has spare parallelism, then flattens once you hit the saturation knee.
Use llama.cpp for one user and vLLM when you serve traffic; the same weights run on both, only the accounting changes.
Quantization is the lever that turns needs-a-datacenter into runs-on-my-desk by storing each weight in fewer bits.
Trained weights cluster near zero, so modern schemes spend their code values where the weights are and protect outlier channels.
The quality cost is not linear: roughly lossless above 8 bits, small near 4 to 6, and steep below 4.
Quality retained holds near full down to about 4 bits, then falls off sharply through Q3 and Q2.
The practical sweet spot for local use is Q4_K_M to Q5_K_M, around 4.5 to 5.5 effective bits per weight.
At a fixed memory budget a 4-bit copy of a bigger model beats an 8-bit copy of a smaller one; parameters buy more than precision.
Everything reduces to one inequality: the model's total footprint must fit under your memory ceiling.
Total inference memory is weights, plus a KV cache that grows with context and batch, plus one to two GB of overhead.

visual.tex: M_{\text{total}} = N_{\text{params}}\tfrac{\text{bpw}}{8} + 2 L\, h_{kv}\, d_k\, T\, b\,\tfrac{\text{bits}_{kv}}{8} + M_{\text{ov}}

beats.text: Weights are constant, but for an 8B model the KV cache climbs steeply with context length.
A 7B model at 4-bit needs about 3.5 GB of weights, plus 1 GB KV and 1.5 GB overhead, for 6 GB total.
That 6 GB fits an 8 GB card with a sliver of headroom, exactly where laptops became viable.
Leave headroom: a model sitting at 100 percent of VRAM will OOM the moment context grows, so target about 85 to 90 percent.
